Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients  | Fruit

The **jackfruit**, also called **jac**, **fenesi**, and **nangka**,*[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)* is a very large fruit from trees found across South and Southeast Asia.[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

One of the largest fruits worldwide,[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2) jackfruit is ovoid in shape and can get up to 80–100 pounds (36–45 kg) and 12–36 x 10–20 inches (30–90 x 25–50 cm).[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) The average is about 10–30 pounds (4.5–14).[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4) The rind of the fruit is thick, bumpy, and green,[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6) turning yellow-brown as it ripens.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7) Underneath the rind are clusters of fleshy "bulbs", each containing a starchy edible seed.[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7) The very center of the fruit consists of an inedible core.[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7) When immature or "green", the jackfruit bulbs are white in color and have a neutral flavor and dense, fibrous texture.[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6)[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) However, as the fruit ripens, the bulbs soften, become more yellow, and develop a sweet, fruity flavor compared to that of bananas, pineapples, and pears.[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2)[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7) As it ripens, it also develops a strong aroma on the outside that some may find unpleasant.[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7)

Jackfruits are sometimes classed as either "hard" or "soft".[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2) The former have very large fruits and firmer flesh, while the latter have smaller fruits, softer flesh, and sweeter flavor.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) Some also name an intermediate variety called "butter" jackfruit.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

* ![Immature jackfruit, sliced up](/wiki/File:696Cusisine_foods_and_delicacies_of_Bulacan_18.jpg "Immature jackfruit, sliced up")

* ![Ripe flesh](/wiki/File:Jackfruit_Flesh.jpg "Ripe flesh")

* ![Seeds](/wiki/File:Artocarpus_heterophyllus_Seeds_(2).jpg "Seeds")

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Jackfruit can be purchased whole, in wedges, extracted from the rind, frozen, and canned.[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6)[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8) Outside of the region of cultivation, it is more commonly found at specialized Asian grocery stores or markets.[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6)[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8) You can identify fully ripe jackfruit from the skin and aroma—the skin will be stretched out with the spikes separating, and the aroma is present but not overpowering.[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2) It may be harvested unripe,[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7) as it will continue to ripen after harvest, even when peeled.[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2) Once ripe, jackfruit no longer keeps very well and is best stored for a couple days in the fridge or a bit longer in the freezer.[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7)

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

If you have purchased a whole jackfruit, it requires some preparation. To start, oil your knife and a large, sharp knife to protect them from the fruit's sticky sap.[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7) Newspaper may also be used to protect your work surface.[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4) The ripe fruit should be cut, lengthwise through the core,[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7) into quarters.[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6) Cut off the white core in the center, then extract the fleshy pods.[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6) Remove seeds and any white fibers from the pods before eating.[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6)[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8) Immature jackfruit can be similarly prepared, but it can also be wedged and sliced up after removing the core and the skin.[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6)

If eating the seeds, remove the white membrane covering the shell before cooking.[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Jackfruit can be eaten as a both ripe and unripe.[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6) Because of its neutral flavor and dense, stringy texture, the unripe fruit is often cooked in savory preparations (e.g. curries),[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8) sometimes as a deliberate meat substitute.[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7) Some liken the texture to that of pulled pork.[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6)[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8) It may also be dried into chips.[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8) Ripe jackfruit is treated more like a culinary fruit,[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4) incorporated into sweet preparations like ice cream, custard, preserves, sticky rice, and more.[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:2-9) Jackfruit shoots and flowers may be eaten as a vegetable.[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2) The seeds are starchy can be roasted or boiled and eaten directly or ground into a flour.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8)

The cuisines of India,[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8) Nepal, Bangladesh,[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8) Sri Lanka, Indonesia, Thailand,[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8) Cambodia, and Vietnam all use jackfruit. In Indonesia, young jackfruit is eaten as gudeg. In many cultures, jackfruit is boiled and used in curries as a staple food.

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

With their neutral flavor and stringy texture, artichoke hearts may be an acceptable substitute for jackfruit.[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8)
